notes:
I think primary research but I found it a bit confusing:
Subjects 
Subjects were PCE referrals from the local DDS, most alleging low cognitive functioning. At the time the DDS claimants signed the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act notification, they were asked for permission to use scores from their examination for research and were assured that their identity would be protected. Permission was granted if the claimant initialized next to the research notification. Only one claimant declined, and her scores were not used. Data were archived and subsequently used for the development of the SVS (Chafetz et al., 2007) and for base-rate research (Chafetz, 2008). The subjects in the TOMM study were 136 Wechsler Adult Intelligence Scale-Third Edition (WAIS-III)-age individuals. Eventually, this writer shifted from the TOMM to the MSVT (Chafetz et al., 2007), and there were 58 WAIS-III-age claimants archived in the MSVT sample. Sample characteristics for the TOMM and MSVT studies are shown in Table 1, and any subject with data missing for the TOMM, MSVT, A-Test, or SVS results has been removed. After missing data were removed, the adult TOMM study contained 113 subjects, and the MSVT study contained 55 subjects.

notes:
Unsure if empirical. Coded just in case
"The current paper reviews efforts within the treatment development literature to handle comorbidity in depressed and anxious children and adolescents, including single-target, modular-based, and transdiagnostic interventions. Two case studies are presented to describe how comorbid anxiety and depression are managed within two different evidence-based single-target interventions. A third case is presented to introduce a novel individual behavioral activation therapy (IBAT) designed to address co-occurring depression and anxiety by targeting a single maintaining mechanism (avoidance) that underlies both disorders."

One of these studies (GENDEP) has an author in common with the common paper while the other (STAR*D) has no authors in common. While the preregistrations to both of these studies are linked to, I'm not sure the preregistrations are valid for the current research. Please note I have answered the remaining preregistration questions about the GENDEP preregistration as this is the primary research aspect. There's a typo in the EudraCT link (should be https://eudract.ema.europa.eu/). However, even correcting for this typo I was unable to find the relevant registration. I was able to access the ISRCTN registration. There was also the following statement at the end of the discussion: “A similar pattern of findings was obtained for the ROA-rated IDS-30 (data available on request).” See data section for quote to support my “limited access” coding. I thought the “upon request” quote looked relevant to other results/data not presented here.

The questions explored the supervisees' experiences of the supervisee-centred way of working, the practical usefulness of this approach, its perceived impact on learning and its perceived usefulness for their future professional lives, and the specific questions asked were as follows: 1. ‘Have you been subjected to this method before, and if so, how’? 2. ‘What have been the pros and cons of supervision based on this working method’? 3. ‘What can be changed to make the method a better tool during the supervision process and work with clients’? 4. ‘How do you think the method has affected your knowledge and skill development compared with previous supervision experiences’? 5. ‘Has the method influenced your approach to learning, and if so, in what way’? 6. ‘If you were to continue to use something from this working method in your profession, what would it be’?

measures_statement_verbatim: The Quantitative consisted of asking service users their agreement with statements on a 5/6 point Likert scale to statements (the higher the score, the higher the level of agreement): · The functional tics group was helpful’ · ‘I learnt a lot from the functional tics group’ · ‘I understand what functional tics are and how they can be managed’ · ‘All my questions were answered by the group’ Duncan et al. 1015 · ‘I would recommend the functional tics group to other young people with functional tics and their parents’ Parents were asked to rate an additional statement: · ‘I feel confident in supporting my child with their functional tics’ Professionals were asked to rate the following statements on a 4-point Likert scale (the higher the score, the more helpful they found the group): · ‘How useful did you find the functional tics psychoeducation group you attended?’ · ‘Did this group cover the topics you thought should be included in a patient psychoeducation group?’ · ‘After attending the group, how confident do you feel offering treatment to a young person with functional tics?’ In addition to collecting Quantitative, open ended questions were asked to parents, young people, and professionals. Open ended questions included: · ‘Please tell us what you enjoyed about the functional tics psychoeducation group’ · ‘Please tell us what you would have improved about the functional tics psychoeducation group’ · ‘What are your thoughts about doing the functional tics psychoeducation group by video?’ · ‘Is there anything else you would like to tell us about the service you received?’ Young people were asked an additional question: · ‘What does your diagnosis mean to you?’ Professionals were asked: · ‘Was there anything that you found useful about joining this group?’ · ‘What could we improve?’ · ‘Do you have any further comments?’ Feedback was gathered via telephone, or via the patient messaging system where necessary.
